title: Were those League Trends a Fluke?
text:
In my first blog (published centuries ago…), I shared some league-wide trends that I found interesting. These trends covered the increased athleticism and kicking performance of starting NFL kickers.
In short…


They are now taller and weigh less



Don’t make them like they used to


Take a moment to appreciate the greatness that was Janikowski



Less field goals (FGs) are being attempted at distances less than 30 yards out


More FGs are being attempted at distances greater than or equal to 50 yards out


Accuracy has increased across the board, regardless the distance


What I didn’t do when exploring these trends is control for a bunch of obvious stuff, like stadium roof type, weather, etc. Why? I wanted to leave you all wanting more.
With all that said, are you ready for this???
LET’S GET TO IT!!!
Quick Note All charts are interactive. Don’t be afraid to play around with them.

title: Were those League Trends a Fluke?
text:
NFL Starter Coverage
So my first blog focused solely on starting kickers. What this means is I carefully determined the starting kickers for each season from 2001 to 2025. How did I do this? If you want to know, I will happily share. To put it simply, I used some machine learning.
More importantly, how well did my approach work?
Looking at the table below, it should apparent that my data accounts for most of the field goals attempted per season. There are some years that are slightly concerning, like 2017 and 2018, but I’m not losing any sleep over it, and neither should you. These years are largely the result of high inconsistency at the starting kicker position. Like, what is going on with 2017? I am so tempted to launch an investigation into that year alone. For starters to account for only 46% of the kickers that year is crazy.
What you need to take away from this is here on out I will make it clear if I am referring all kickers or only starters. This distinction is important. Keep an eye out for it.


Download CSV


* FGA stands for Field Goals Attempted.

title: Were those League Trends a Fluke?
text:
The Obvious
Did you watch SpongeBob as a kid? Do you kids watch SpongeBob? That freakin’ sponge will never go away. He’s like the damn gum on the bottom of your shoe (haha). There was an episode where he became a hermit and feared going outside. Sandy eventually convinced him to come outdoors, but shortly after, he was mangled by a gorilla.
The point is, chilling out inside is undeniably nice sometimes, especially when winter is here. It’s not far fetched to think that kickers feel the same way. Let’s take a look at the trends in FG attempts, FG%, and the league median FG distance over the years when kicking inside compared to outside. These trends were calculated using all NFL kickers.
The charts tell us three key points.


There have been more FG attempts outside than inside, but the number of FG attempts inside is slowly catching up (more on this later)


For most years, FG% tends to be better inside than outside


The league median kick distance inside is usually greater than that outside - makes sense








Let’s focus on FG attempts. Do our eyes deceive us? Are they really catching up to the number of FGs attempted outside? Yeah, they are - the stats clearly support this narrative.








Indoor vs. Outdoor FG Attempts (2001–2025)


Chi-squared Test for Trend in Proportions


Chi-squared
p-value
df




267.12
4.82 × 10−60
1







This result raises another important question. Is the league transitioning toward more stadiums that are, or can potentially be, indoors?
Oddly enough, the number of domes has shockingly decreased. BUT… there are now more stadiums with retractable roofs. Even though the total number of stadiums that are, or can potentially be, indoors has NOT increased, the number of stadiums with retractable roofs has. Again, the stats support this narrative, and it could be one reason why the number of FGs attempted inside has also increased.














Indoor Stadium Growth (2001–2025)


Chi-squared Test for Trend in Proportions


Chi-squared
p-value
df




2.16
0.14132
1















Retractable Roof Stadium Growth (2001–2025)


Chi-squared Test for Trend in Proportions


Chi-squared
p-value
df




16.54
4.75 × 10−5
1







What does this trend mean for kicking performance? Does the stadium roof type really matter? Are the distance and success of any given kick actually impacted by whether it’s attempted inside or outside?
According to the stats, FG kicks attempted inside have a higher conversion rate and are roughly 1 yard longer than those outside. Cool, so they both are. I guess being an indoor hermit ain’t that bad.








FG Result by Roof Type (2001–2025)


Chi-squared Test of Independence


Chi-squared
p-value
df




17.63
2.69 × 10−5
1















Kick Distance by Roof Type (2001–2025)


Mann-Whitney U Test of Distributional Shift


Locational Difference
W
p-value
CI Lower
CI Higher




1.0000
62237579
1.22 × 10−13
0.9999
1.0001







Did I mislead everyone by reporting a bunch of things in the first blog without controlling for the obvious? Long story short, nah, but it’s more nuanced than I suggested.
The FG% outside is increasing by 0.29% per season, whereas the FG% inside is increasing by 0.13% per season. Overall, there is an undoubted steady increase in FG% per season since 2001, but this rate of increase depends heavily on whether the kick is inside or outside. The faster rate of increase per season for outside kicks is actually remarkable when you think about it.
Is it because of improved coaching, training, or athleticism? You can really go down some serious rabbit holes with that one. Getting back on track, let’s look at the league median kick distance.








Impact of Being Inside on FG%


Outdoor Accuracy is Getting Scary Good!

Model Fit: R² = 0.523 | Adj. R² = 0.492 | F-statistic = 16.83 (df = 46) | p = <0.001


CI stands for Confidence Interval














Despite no interaction between the stadium roof type and the season, the results clearly indicate that the league median kick distance is increasing at a rate of 0.153 yards per season, regardless of whether a game is played indoors or outdoors. Moreover, indoor games have a consistent 0.86-yard advantage in median kick distance over outdoor games, but both settings are experiencing the same annual increase in median kick distance.
As of right now, this model is a sham. It couldn’t predict the outcome of a coin flip, so let’s take a look at the weather. Is Mother Nature really that scary?














Model Comparison: League Median Kick Distance


Evaluating the necessity of Roof Type and Interaction effects


Predictor

Partial (No Interaction)
Full (Interaction)




Baseline (Outside)

Yearly Trend (Yds/Year)
0.153 (p=<0.01)
0.153 (p=<0.01)
0.145 (p=<0.01)

Note:Estimates shown with p-values in parentheses. The high p-value for the ’Interaction’suggests it does not significantly improve the model.


F-Tests:Partial vs Null (Roof Effect): p = 0.0038125Full vs Partial (Interaction): p = 0.69179








Before I get into anything, here’s how I broke down the weather for each outdoor game. If a game had any of the following, it was deemed as having bad weather or not ideal kicking conditions.


Precipitation (rain and snow)


Extreme cold (temperatures colder than 96% of all games)


Extreme heat (temperatures hotter than 96% of all games)


Every other outdoor game should have at least decent weather. Since we’re talking about NFL kickers, decent weather should be good enough. Right?
Looking at the chart below, it’s clear my approach is conservative, as most games seem to have decent or good weather. After all, we are talking about humans, too. Safety comes first, folks.
It is interesting to see the decrease in bad weather games. Again, this could be due to the league trending toward more retractable stadiums.






How do FG% and the league median kick distance stack up?
It turns out Mother Nature is unbiased. All her wrath was directed toward FG%. Good weather games, unsurprisingly, tend to have a higher seasonal FG% compared to bad weather games. Moreover, there is seemingly zero effect of weather on the league’s median kick distance - more on this later.
But before we jump to any conclusions, let’s see what the stats have to say.






It turns out the seasonal increase in FG% is really robust! Let’s go!
If I am going to be technically correct…
Even if the weather stayed the same from year to year, we’d still see FG% climb about 0.3% every season. On top of that, playing in good weather effectively gives kickers a 2.1% accuracy increase over games played in the elements. Interesting, but nothing groundbreaking to report here.

Mother Nature Means Business!

F-Tests:Partial vs Null (Roof Effect): p = 0.048309Full vs Partial (Interaction): p = 0.79616








Moving on to the league median kick distance, the stats suggest that our eyes did not deceive us. For only outdoor kicks, it is unfazed by the weather. I’m gonna guess that many of you think this result is underwhelming.
WRONG
This result couldn’t be more exciting because it means we can include both these terms in the model without unwanted effects. If you’re a stats nerd, then you know I am referring to the curse of multicollinearity.
Are you ready for what’s next? ARE YOU READY????
From here on out, we are really going to start really shifting gears and picking up the pace. These blogs will focus on building a robust model to predict the success of any given FG kick.
So far, we’ve analyzed the impact of the following on the seasonal trends in FG% and league median kick distance.


Season


Stadium Roof Type


Weather


I’ve carefully structured the model so that the impact of weather on any given FG kick is completely removed from the prediction for indoor kicks. If you are interested in how I did this, let me know! I’d be happy to explain.

title: Were those League Trends a Fluke?
text:
Time To Get Serious
We can clearly see that all terms in the model are statistically significant for predicting the success of any given FG kick. This is what you should take away from the results below.


For every 1-yard increase in kick distance, the odds of a make decrease by 10.1%


If we were to kick at the same distance every time, kicking outdoors in good weather reduces the odds of making a kick by 21.6% compared to kicking inside


If we are kicking outside, bad weather further reduces the odds of making a kick by an additional 16% compared to kicking in good weather


In other words, kicking outside in bad weather reduces the odds of making a kick by about 34% compared to kicking inside at the same distance. Hahaha holy crap that’s a lot!!!













FG Probability: Environmental & Distance Model


Predicting the Odds of a Successful Conversion


Predictor
Odds Ratio (p-value)
95% CI




Baseline (Inside)
283.974 (p=< 0.001)
[232.534, 347.709]


Kick Distance (per Yard)
0.899 (p=< 0.001)
[0.896, 0.903]


Yearly Trend
1.041 (p=< 0.001)
[1.035, 1.046]


Outside Effect (Good Weather)
0.784 (p=< 0.001)
[0.72, 0.854]


Bad Weather Effect (Outside Kicks Only)
0.841 (p=0.00673)
[0.742, 0.954]



Note: Values are exponentiated Odds Ratios. An OR < 1 indicates a decrease in the odds of success. The Bad Weather Effect is conditional; it only applies to outside kicks.








If we focus on the 2025 season, we can even plot FG success probability curves for inside kicks, outside kicks in good weather, and outside kicks in bad weather. Look at the curve for outside kicks! What a drop off…
We can go even further and determine which kickers performed over the league average expectation in 2025. First, we gotta predict whether each kick should have been made or missed. Summing these probabilities over the 2025 season tells us how many kicks each kicker should have made. Subtracting this expectation from their actual total tells us who over- and underachieved.
Will Reichard is a freakin’ beast… He accounted for four more FGs than an average league kicker if that kicker were given the same kicking conditions. His value to the Vikings is stupid. Twelve points can win a game in some match-ups.
Cough… cough… Denver vs LV in Week 10…
Check out the leaderboard here if you’re interested -> Leaderboard

title: Were those League Trends a Fluke?
text:
Summary
Seems like a lot happened in this blog, so it’d be good to summarize everything before closing out.


Do starting kickers account for most of the FGs attempted in any given season?



Yeah… duh…



Are really getting more accurate as the season march on?



Absolutely



Are longer kicks a mirage?



No way, kick distance is increasing annually.



How does kicking fare inside vs. outside?



Clearly SpongeBob knew something about the great outdoors.



Why does every team seem to be getting a new stadium?



Cause they are, and they’re all retractable. Sofi started a trend.



How is Mother Nature treating outside FG attempts?



She clearly has some issues with kickers.


Their accuracy is taking quite a hit in bad weather games.


But they’re not afraid because they’re still kicking from insane distances.



Who was the last year’s Most Valuable Kicker (MVK)?



Give Will Reichard a round of applause!


He’s currently atop the leaderboard, but it’s gonna be fun to see how this changes as our model gets more accurate.

title: Were those League Trends a Fluke?
text:
Closing
If you thought that was cool, keep thinking. We are just getting started. In the next blog, I am going to take a look at how all the following impact FG% and the league median kick distance. Those that do will be added to our model to adjust the rankings.


Time zone changes


Altitude


Back-to-back divisional games


Back-to-back games against opponents with records above .500

title: League-Wide Trends in NFL Kicking
text:
Why Kickers?
I’ve been involved with football my entire life, all the way from player and fan to only fan. Terrell Davis was my inspiration as I sought to be an NFL running back. However, I don’t think I ever started a game. Being a backup, I was a kicker’s best friend. Who do you think caught all the balls during punt and kickoff practice?
As a fan, if there is one thing that I’ve noticed watching games recently, it’s that kickers are becoming focal points of the league. Think about it. Whenever a game is coming down to the wire, and it’s only a 1 or 2 point difference, what is the first thing that pops into your head? Field goal.
Whenever your team loses by 1 or 2 points, and your kicker missed a key field goal and/or extra point during the game, who do you blame? The kicker.
I harp on the negatives because humans are inclined to remember the bad times. So to brighten the mood, is there a better feeling than seeing your team’s kicker hit a game-winning field goal? No. It’s even better when the game is a back-and-forth battle, or when your team seals the deal to cap off an incredible comeback. There is something special about that minute before the kick when it’s deadpan silence and everyone is holding their breath. It’s captivating. It’s emotional. NFL kicking is quickly becoming one of the best parts of the league.
This year we witnessed Cam Little make history. He hit a 70 yard field goal. After watching it on repeat, I decided to write about kickers. Bottom-line, I hate them in fantasy football, but they’re becoming more important than ever.

title: League-Wide Trends in NFL Kicking
text:
Setting the Stage
To begin this journey into the game’s third easiest scapegoat, I start with league-wide trends. These are the questions I hope to answer in this blog.

Are kickers getting more athletic?
Are they getting more chances to kick?
Has the league’s distance per field goal increased?
Are they getting more accurate, and are they getting more accurate from further out?

Did I analyze EVERY kicker signed by an NFL team over the past 25 years to draw my conclusions? Nope. I removed one-offs from the data by focusing on only those kickers that met my per-season thresholds for field goal attempts.
With all that behind us, here we go…

title: League-Wide Trends in NFL Kicking
text:
Kicker Demographics
It feels like yesterday when the league record for a field goal was 63-yards. Now that record is seemingly smashed every year.
Is it because kickers nowadays are all 6 feet tall, lean, and jacked. Is the wimpiest guy on the team now an athletic freak? Looking at the trends in median height, weight, and age, the data suggest this is the case. It’s clear that kickers have gotten taller and leaner.
It’s not surprising to see only a one inch difference in height over the last 25 years. If the current median height of an NFL kicker were 6’ 5”, I’m sure they’d be trying their hand at a different position or sport.
Were you thinking basketball? lol
The drop in weight is the most surprising trend. A ten pound difference in weight is both statistically, and biologically, significant. The lighter and leaner kickers of the modern day NFL aren’t just a perception, they’re a reality. They are becoming athletic freaks. It’d be cool to see if this results in more fakes on special teams.
Before I move on, it is a bummer to see that the median age of NFL kickers hasn’t changed much. My first thought was greater athleticism could be explained by a youth movement at the position.
The Aussie Advent is my second guess. More and more kickers entering the league are Australian. It could be Australia’s dedication to growing the skill of the position. They have youth camps and kicking programs that many current Australian NFL kickers spent their entire childhood within. This line of investigation deserves way more research.


Download CSV










Now that we have established that the data suggests modern day NFL kickers are becoming athletic freaks, let’s dive into kicking related trends across the league.

title: League-Wide Trends in NFL Kicking
text:
Kicking Trends
Whether you’re looking at the table or the charts, it is cut and dry that NFL kickers are getting more field goal attempts than ever. This is evident from the statistical significance of the league median field goal attempts (median_fga) per season. It’s growing at a yearly rate of 0.132 attempts on average since 2001. I’m not going to ignore the 2017 season. It could be the outlier driving this trend. I mean, a difference of 1 between 2001 and 2025 isn’t convincing.
However, a quick sensitivity analysis reveals that this trend is robust.
Interestingly, if you probe deeper into this finding, kickers are getting less attempts at distances less than 30 yards out and more attempts at distances greater than 50 yards out. This makes sense when you consider there has also been a steady increase in the league median kick distance (median_kick_dst) per field goal attempt.
So, kickers are getting more field goal attempts than ever. Got it. Combine this with less attempts at distances less than 30 yards out and more attempts at distances greater than 50 yards, and we see a greater league median kick distance per field goal attempt. That’s a mouth full, but I think you get the picture.
Before I move on, I’m going to say that again. Kickers are getting less attempts at distances less than 30 yards out and more attempts at distances greater than 50 yards out…
Doesn’t that sound weird?
After hearing it, I would think kickers are getting less accurate. Think about it. Closer field goals are easier to convert. Accuracy is mathematically calculated as conversions divided by attempts. Therefore, closer field goals also pad stats, like field goal percentage. Right?
Turns out the data suggests that kickers are getting more accurate. In fact, the league average field goal percentage has steadily increased over the past 25 years, regardless the distance.
This is incredible. It’s beautifully captured by the heat map, especially when you focus on the row that represents kicking distances 60 yards and out. It just gets lighter and lighter as you move from the left to the right. This means it’s getting better.


Download CSV

title: League-Wide Trends in NFL Kicking
text:
Summary
That was a lot of info, so let’s recap.

Are kickers getting more athletic?

The data suggests this is case given they are getting taller and leaner.

Are they getting more chances to kick?

Kickers are indeed getting more chances to kick.

Has the league’s distance per field goal increased?

You bet. In fact, they’re getting more attempts at distances 50 yards and out.

Are they getting more accurate, and are they getting more accurate from further out?

Yes and yes. Not only are they getting more accurate overall, the increase in accuracy at distances 50 yards and out is the most notable.


That seems like a lot, but I’m just getting started. Next week I’m diving into kicking at the team level, so stay tuned.
